steps.title: Read the radius from standard form
steps.content: In the circle equation, the right side equals $r^2$. Read circle M's radius from its equation first, then double that radius for circle N.

steps.title: Identify the radius of circle M
steps.content: The equation of circle M is $(x-4)^2 + (y+6)^2 = 36$. This is in standard form $(x-h)^2 + (y-k)^2 = r^2$, where the right side equals $r_M^2$. Therefore $r_M^2 = 36$, giving us $r_M = 6$.

steps.title: Calculate k for circle N using the radius relationship
steps.content: Circle N has radius twice that of circle M, so $r_N = 2 \cdot r_M = 2 \cdot 6 = 12$. The equation for circle N is $(x-4)^2 + (y+6)^2 = k$, where $k$ represents $r_N^2$. Therefore: $$k = 12^2 = 144$$
